text: The surgeon performs a left-sided resection, beginning with the ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery and vein. Dissection starts at the sacral promontory with an incision in the parietal peritoneum. The inferior mesenteric artery is dissected using a combination of sharp and blunt techniques, secured with Hem-o-lok clips, and divided, opening the medial to lateral plane.

The surgeon continues dissection between the mesocolon and Girotta's fascia, progressing cranially to the lower border of the pancreas. A swab is placed to aid in flexure mobilization. The inferior mesenteric vein is divided between clips, completing the medial to lateral dissection. The remaining thin layer of lateral attachments of the colon is released using hook endothermy.

The patient is positioned in reverse Trendelenburg for splenic flexure mobilization. The surgeon begins with gastrocolic omental dissection and releases the transverse colon using a harmonic scalpel to dissect the root of the transverse mesocolon. The swab placed above the pancreas is visible. Laterally, the colon is fully mobilized by dividing the renocolic ligament.

The patient is positioned in Trendelenburg with a left tilt for the aliocolic vascular dissection. The duodenum is identified as a key landmark for safely dividing the vascular pedicle. The aliocolic vessels are secured with hemlock clips and divided, and a swab is placed above the duodenum at the end of this stage.

The surgeon mobilizes the right colon using hook diathermy, applying traction and counter-traction for precise dissection. The hepatic flexure is mobilized by dividing the gastrocolic omentum, with the swab above the duodenum visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon manages the middle colic vessels by dissecting from both the right and left sides. An assistant lifts the mesocolon to facilitate the separation and division of the right and left branches of the mesocolic vessels, which are secured with hemolog clips. At the end of the dissection, the entire colon is visibly free from attachments.

The surgeon performs a proctectomy in the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, beginning posteriorly behind the superior rectal artery. Dithermy is used for precise dissection in front of the presacral fascia, preserving the hypogastric nerves. Anterior dissection is marked at the peritoneal reflection level, with a plaget providing counter traction. Dissection proceeds circumferentially down to the pelvic floor, mobilizing the rectum by releasing the left lateral sidewall. Anterior dissection continues within the Denonvilliers fascia to protect the nerves.

The surgeon uses proline sutures to hitch up the peritoneal reflection, enhancing access and visibility. Dissection proceeds anteriorly behind the seminal vesicles using a harmonic scalpel, completing the dissection at the pelvic floor level. The rectal tube is dissected.

The surgeon performs a rectal examination to assess the level of rectal transection. The rectum is divided anterior-posteriorly using a roticulating endo-GIA stapler, with two firings of a forty-five millimeter stapler.

The surgeon extracts the specimen through the umbilicus and constructs an ileal J pouch using a linear cutter stapler, requiring two to three firings to achieve a fifteen-centimeter pouch. A CDH-29 circular stapler is used for the ileoanal anastomosis, performed under direct vision. The small bowel is oriented correctly by following the line of the small bowel mesentery from the duodenal jugular flexure to the ileoanal anastomosis.
